intro: A census in the Old Testament was a count of the male population, primarily for military assessment and taxation, and carried significant theological implications when undertaken without divine sanction. Scripture records five principal census events among the Israelites. (1.) The Sinai census at the beginning of the wilderness wanderings numbered 603,550 men of fighting age (Exodus 38:26; Numbers 1:46). (2.) A second census at the close of the wilderness period counted 601,730 (Numbers 26:51), showing a slight decrease. (3.) David's census near the end of his reign, which he ordered against Joab's counsel, provoked divine wrath because it reflected proud self-reliance rather than trust in God's protection; a plague killed seventy thousand men before God relented (2 Samuel 24; 1 Chronicles 21). (4.) Solomon enrolled the alien residents in Israel, numbering 153,600 (2 Chronicles 2:17). (5.) In the New Testament, the census of Augustus Caesar that brought Joseph and Mary to Bethlehem for the birth of Jesus (Luke 2:1–7) is a pivotal event connecting Roman imperial administration to the fulfillment of Micah's prophecy (Micah 5:2).
